context.story_engine: An antisocial maverick doctor uses unconventional methods to solve puzzling medical cases that other physicians cannot diagnose

plotlines.name: Case of the Week
plotlines.goal: diagnose and treat mysterious medical cases that other physicians cannot solve
plotlines.obstacle: complex symptoms, misleading evidence, patient deception, time pressure

plotlines.name: House: Authority
plotlines.goal: maintain autonomy in his medical practice and resist administrative control
plotlines.obstacle: Cuddy's clinic duties, Vogler's corporate demands, institutional pressure
plotlines.stakes: loss of medical license, termination, inability to practice medicine his way

plotlines.name: Vogler: Control
plotlines.goal: transform the hospital into a profitable biotech venture and eliminate House's department
plotlines.obstacle: House's resistance, Cuddy's protection, board opposition
plotlines.stakes: loss of investment, failure to modernize hospital

plotlines.name: House: Past
plotlines.goal: navigate his unresolved feelings for ex-girlfriend Stacy
plotlines.obstacle: Stacy is married, professional boundaries, emotional vulnerability

plotlines.name: House: Addiction
plotlines.goal: manage his Vicodin dependency while functioning as a doctor
plotlines.obstacle: withdrawal symptoms, impaired judgment, physical dependence

events.event: Rebecca Adler collapses from a seizure in her classroom and loses her ability to speak
House initially refuses the case
Wilson claims Rebecca is his cousin to get House to take the case
Cuddy tries to force House to fulfill his clinic duties
House refuses clinic duties and has his MRI authorization revoked as punishment
House diagnoses Rebecca with cerebral vasculitis and begins treatment
Rebecca's condition initially improves with treatment
House convinces Foreman to break into Rebecca's house to investigate
Rebecca suddenly loses her vision and suffers another seizure
Foreman discovers ham in Rebecca's house, revealing Wilson's lie
The ham discovery leads House to suspect tapeworms as the cause
Rebecca refuses treatment for neurocysticercosis
House has Chase perform a non-invasive X-ray that reveals a tapeworm larva in Rebecca's leg
Rebecca agrees to treatment and recovers

episodes.theme: the necessity of breaking rules to save lives

interactions.description: both plotlines explore House's willingness to break rules—medical protocol for diagnosis, administrative rules for autonomy

events.event: Dan's parents bring him to House after receiving Cameron's letter
House starts a bet with his team about Dan's biological parentage
Dan's condition worsens with myoclonic twitches and blocked blood vessel
House makes an initial diagnosis based on Dan's symptoms
Dan suffers auditory hallucination during procedure, ruling out House's diagnosis
House secretly performs unauthorized paternity test using coffee cups
House discovers neither parent is biologically related to Dan
House recalls previous case involving vaccination refusal
House theorizes Dan contracted measles virus during childhood
House confirms subacute sclerosing panencephalitis with retinal biopsy
House successfully treats Dan

episodes.theme: the hidden truths that shape our lives

events.event: Brandon collapses after having sex with his fiancée and presents with numerous seemingly unrelated symptoms
Foreman proposes a diagnosis arguing it follows Occam's razor
House proposes a competing diagnosis also claiming it follows Occam's razor
Brandon's white blood cell count drops dramatically
Both Foreman's and House's theories are proven wrong by the blood test results
At the clinic pharmacy, House theorizes the pharmacist accidentally dispensed colchicine instead of cough medicine
House administers the antidote and Brandon begins recovering immediately
The pharmacist denies making any mistake
Brandon mentions his old cough medicine didn't have letters on the pills like his current medication
House discovers colchicine pills that closely resemble cough medicine tablets, confirming the accidental poisoning

episodes.theme: the danger of assumptions and the importance of looking beyond the obvious

events.event: House overhears a conversation about a sick baby and investigates the maternity ward
House predicts an epidemic in the maternity ward
Cuddy quarantines the entire maternity ward after confirming the outbreak severity
House begins treating the affected children
Two babies develop kidney failure
House tests which drug caused the kidney failure complication
One infant dies during the drug testing
Autopsy reveals echovirus 11, CMV, and parvovirus B19 antibodies in the deceased baby
The team tests the mothers and identifies echovirus as the epidemic's cause
House uses experimental antiviral treatment to successfully cure the remaining babies
House discovers an elderly hospital volunteer who has been coughing and wiping her nose while distributing toys
House identifies the volunteer as the source of contamination

episodes.theme: the cost of medical decisions and unintended consequences

events.event: Sister Augustine arrives at the hospital with severe hand rashes that her fellow nuns believe resemble stigmata
House diagnoses contact dermatitis from dish soap allergy and prescribes antihistamines
The medication triggers an asthma attack, prompting House to administer epinephrine
Augustine suffers a minor heart attack from the epinephrine
House's team suspects he made an error with the epinephrine dosage
Augustine develops new symptoms including hallucinations, convulsions, and leg rashes
Cuddy removes House from the case due to his controversial methods
Investigation of the convent reveals figwort tea, which explains only the epinephrine reaction
Despite being placed in a hypoallergenic room, Augustine continues having allergic reactions
Augustine begins shouting about having God inside her
House realizes she has a copper IUD that's causing the allergic responses
The device is surgically removed and Augustine fully recovers

episodes.theme: the conflict between faith and reason in understanding the human condition

interactions.description: both plotlines explore the tension between unconventional methods and institutional control

events.event: Lucille Palmeiro collapses when a blood clot travels from her leg to her heart
Lucille begins vomiting blood at the hospital
House suspects vitamin K deficiency based on the bleeding
The team discovers unused ampicillin and frozen microwave dinners in Lucille's home
An ultrasound reveals liver cirrhosis and a cancerous tumor too large for surgical removal
House orders ethanol injection to shrink the tumor, making surgery possible
Lucille makes an unexpected decision to call Social Services to remove her son
House claims Lucille's decision is inconsistent with her supposed schizophrenia
The team considers Wilson's disease as an explanation for all symptoms
An eye exam reveals copper-colored rings around Lucille's irises, confirming Wilson's disease
Lucille receives treatment for Wilson's disease and recovers completely
Lucille reunites with her son

episodes.theme: the complexity of diagnosis requires looking beyond surface symptoms to find the underlying truth

events.event: Ed returns from jogging with his best friend to find his bedridden wife Elise behaving uncharacteristically abusive toward him
House concludes Elise either has rabbit fever from cooking rabbits or African sleeping sickness
House notes that sleeping sickness would require sexual transmission since neither has traveled to Africa
Both Ed and Elise vehemently deny having affairs
House begins treating Elise for rabbit fever
The rabbit fever treatment fails
House successfully treats Elise for sleeping sickness
Under pressure of her diagnosis and treatment, Elise confesses to having an affair with her husband's best friend
Ed leaves Elise despite her recovery

episodes.theme: the truth that medicine reveals can destroy as much as it heals

events.event: House investigates the mysterious poisoning of high school student Matt Davis
Another teenager arrives with identical symptoms but no apparent connection to Matt
House must find the common link between the two cases
The team discovers both teens purchased inexpensive jeans at a car boot sale
The team determines the jeans were exposed to high doses of pesticides, causing phosdrin poisoning
House treats an elderly woman at the clinic who has been flirting with him
House diagnoses the elderly woman with neurosyphilis

episodes.theme: hidden connections reveal dangerous truths

interactions.description: both plotlines involve House uncovering hidden medical truths through careful investigation

events.event: John Henry Giles, a legendary jazz musician with ALS, is admitted for pneumonia treatment under Foreman's care
House attempts to prove John Henry doesn't actually have ALS
House's intervention causes respiratory failure in John Henry
House intubates John Henry in violation of his DNR order
House uses a legal technicality to keep John Henry on life support
Cameron discovers a blood clot in John Henry
The blood clot is removed from John Henry
An MRI reveals an arteriovenous malformation in John Henry
Surgery to correct the malformation restores John Henry's ability to walk
House's theory is proven correct - John Henry never had ALS
Foreman receives a lucrative job offer from John Henry's doctor
Foreman learns the job offer is from his former mentor

episodes.theme: the cost of being right when everyone else is wrong

interactions.description: both plotlines explore House's willingness to break rules and defy authority to achieve what he believes is right

events.event: Foreman believes the homeless woman is faking seizures for food and shelter
Wilson becomes invested in the homeless woman's case to prevent her falling through healthcare cracks
House uses the case to teach two medical students about conducting patient histories
The team diagnoses the woman with tuberculoma and rabies
The team discovers the rabies is too advanced for treatment
The patient bites Foreman during treatment
Foreman must undergo rabies prophylaxis after being bitten
Foreman and Wilson search for the patient's family
They discover she became homeless after losing her husband and son in a car accident two years earlier
Foreman pretends to be her deceased husband and offers her forgiveness as she dies
The homeless woman dies

episodes.theme: compassion and the human cost of medical judgment

events.event: A young patient won't stop bleeding after a car accident with his girlfriend
House accepts Cuddy's challenge to go off Vicodin for a week in exchange for no clinic duty for a month
House's withdrawal symptoms intensify
House's diagnostic methods become increasingly harsh and risky
Foreman and Cameron worry about House's judgment
House decides to exhume the family's recently deceased cat
House performs an autopsy on the cat
House discovers high levels of naphthalene from termite excretion in the cat
House reveals the patient has been exposed to poisonous vapors from a termite nest behind his bedroom walls

episodes.theme: the cost of brilliance

interactions.description: both plotlines explore how House's methods—medical and personal—are simultaneously destructive and effective
House's withdrawal symptoms directly affect his ability to diagnose the patient, making his medical judgment more erratic yet ultimately successful

events.event: Major league pitcher Hank Wiggen suffers a severely broken arm that reveals bizarre bone loss
House suspects Hank is lying about steroid use given his history of drug abuse
Hank's condition deteriorates
Hank's kidneys begin failing
Hank's wife offers to donate one of her kidneys but would have to abort her early pregnancy
Hank opposes his wife's kidney donation because it requires aborting the pregnancy
House discovers that Hank's wife has lost her sense of smell
House realizes both have smoked cannabis grown on cadmium-polluted soil, causing cadmium poisoning
Foreman dates a pharmaceutical representative
House attends a monster truck rally with Cameron

episodes.theme: the hidden costs of choices and deception

interactions.description: the case explores how past choices (drug use, environmental exposure) create present consequences that force impossible decisions

events.event: A young boy consults a Ouija board and believes he will die
The boy is hospitalized with pneumonia
Chase's estranged father arrives at the hospital
Chase's father assists House's team with the diagnosis
House becomes suspicious of the seemingly random visit
House questions Chase's father about his visit
Chase's father reveals he has terminal cancer
The team diagnoses the boy with both anthrax and leprosy
Chase and his father briefly reconcile
Chase's father departs for the airport, leaving their relationship somewhat resolved

episodes.theme: confronting mortality and unfinished relationships

interactions.description: both the boy's fear of death from the Ouija board and Chase's father's terminal cancer explore themes of mortality and facing death

events.event: Edward Vogler donates $100 million to Princeton-Plainsboro and becomes Chairman of the Board
Vogler plans to transform the clinic into a profitable biotech venture and eliminate House's department
A successful businesswoman arrives with inexplicable paralysis despite her seemingly perfect life and health
House begins investigating the businesswoman's paralysis symptoms
House diagnoses the businesswoman with congestive heart failure caused by bulimia and regular ipecac use
Vogler opposes House's methods and threatens his department
House treats the patient despite Vogler's opposition, putting his job and medical license at risk
House's defiance of Vogler escalates the conflict over his autonomy

episodes.theme: the cost of integrity versus institutional power

interactions.description: House's treatment of the patient directly conflicts with Vogler's authority, merging the medical case with the power struggle
both plotlines explore the theme of doing what's right despite external pressure and personal cost

events.event: House is court-ordered to diagnose mobster Joey Arnello
Joey is scheduled for federal testimony and entry into Witness Protection
Joey's brother, a lawyer, works against the medical team
The team diagnoses Joey with Hepatitis C
Joey's brother works against the testimony when Joey is diagnosed with Hepatitis C
The team discovers Joey has ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency
Cuddy battles Vogler over House's value to the hospital
Vogler becomes increasingly determined to eliminate House's department

episodes.theme: the conflict between institutional control and individual autonomy

interactions.description: both plotlines involve external forces trying to control House's medical practice—the court system and Vogler's corporate agenda
Vogler's campaign against House directly threatens House's autonomy and medical practice

events.event: An overweight ten-year-old girl suffers a heart attack and is brought to the hospital
The girl's mother insists the team look beyond her weight for the real diagnosis
Vogler demands House fire a team member
House discovers that Chase has been feeding information about his cases to Vogler
House tells Cuddy and Vogler he chooses to fire Chase
Vogler rejects House's decision and demands House fire either Foreman or Cameron instead
The team diagnoses the girl with Cushing's disease, explaining both her weight and cardiac symptoms

episodes.theme: the cost of loyalty and betrayal

interactions.description: all three plotlines explore looking beyond surface appearances—the girl's weight masks Cushing's disease, Chase's loyalty masks betrayal, House's defiance masks his care for his team
Vogler's demand for House to fire someone directly challenges House's autonomy, forcing both conflicts into the same confrontation

events.event: A popular U.S. senator and presidential candidate collapses at a fundraiser
Vogler assigns House to the high-profile senator case
Vogler offers to let House keep his entire team if he endorses Vogler's pharmaceutical company in a speech
The senator's symptoms initially suggest AIDS
House investigates deeper into the senator's condition
House deduces that Chase is Vogler's informant within his team
During his required speech, House insults Vogler and his company instead of endorsing them
House's speech reignites his conflict with Vogler
Cameron quits the team in response to the ongoing political pressure
House treats a woman who claims to be pregnant without having sex
The senator is diagnosed with toxoplasmosis and delayed-onset CVID secondary to phenytoin-mediated Epstein-Barr virus infection

episodes.theme: the cost of integrity in corrupt systems

interactions.description: all plotlines explore deception and hidden truths—the senator's mysterious illness, Chase's betrayal, and House's defiant honesty
House's speech directly confronts Vogler, merging House's resistance with Vogler's corporate agenda

events.event: House informs Chase and Foreman that Cameron has quit
A pregnant woman arrives with brain and kidney problems
The team discovers she has small cell lung cancer
She and her husband must choose between her life and their unborn child's survival
Vogler proposes the board revoke House's tenure and fire him
Wilson opposes the motion
Vogler proposes removing Wilson from the board, which passes
Wilson resigns in protest
Cuddy challenges Vogler's control over the board
Four other board members support Cuddy
Vogler is forced to leave as chairman and revoke his $100 million donation
The team celebrates Vogler's departure
Cuddy reminds House the conflict was unnecessary
House treats a malnourished baby whose vegan parents are arrested for child endangerment
A CT scan reveals the baby has DiGeorge syndrome, exonerating the parents

episodes.theme: the cost of standing up to authority

interactions.description: both plotlines explore making difficult choices to protect what matters most - Cuddy/Wilson protecting House, parents protecting their child
Vogler's defeat resolves both his campaign for control and House's struggle against administrative authority

events.event: House fights a meningitis outbreak at the hospital
House identifies a young patient who doesn't fit typical meningitis criteria
House gives his team one hour to produce diagnostic results
House attempts to convince Cameron to return following Vogler's departure
Cameron demands House explain why he really wants her back
House and Wilson interview potential replacements for Cameron's position
The young patient is diagnosed with thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura secondary to pregnancy
Cameron agrees to return to the team in exchange for House taking her on a dinner date

episodes.theme: the price of getting what you want

interactions.description: both plotlines involve House having to work within constraints and make compromises to achieve his goals

events.event: Hospital staff gossip about House's upcoming date with Cameron
House treats a clinic patient harshly
The clinic patient develops a mysterious stroke
House diagnoses the stroke as fulminating osteomyelitis
House encounters an elderly couple with medical problems from overactive sex life
House navigates his complicated personal dynamics with Cameron regarding their date
The two cases interweave as House works to solve both medical mysteries
House resolves the medical cases through his diagnostic insights

episodes.theme: the intersection of professional duty and personal relationships

interactions.description: both medical cases involve intimate relationships affecting health, mirroring House's own complicated personal dynamics

events.event: Stacy Warner visits House seeking help for her husband Mark's medical condition
Cuddy forces House to lecture medical students on patient diagnosis
House presents three scenarios involving different causes of leg pain to students
House discusses farmer with streptococcal infection case
House presents volleyball player with osteosarcoma case
House reveals his own past case of thigh muscle infarction
House covers Professor Riley's lead paint poisoning case
House reflects on his diagnostic methodology during the lecture
House deals with Stacy's unexpected return to his life

episodes.theme: the intersection of professional duty and personal history

interactions.description: both plotlines explore how past experiences shape present decisions and medical practice
House's personal leg injury case becomes part of his teaching, merging his professional and personal history
both involve House being forced to confront things he'd rather avoid

events.event: Mark presents with stomach pain while tests show no clear condition
House discovers Mark's brain appears to be deteriorating
House initially diagnoses abdominal epilepsy but cannot detect memory loss
Mark develops paralysis
House treats Mark for Guillain-Barré syndrome
House confides in Stacy that he still has feelings for her
House realizes Mark has been experiencing delusions and suffers from acute intermittent porphyria
House's team opposes his dangerous drug cocktail treatment plan
Stacy supports House's dangerous treatment plan despite the risks
House administers the dangerous drug cocktail to confirm AIP diagnosis
The treatment succeeds and Mark recovers
Cuddy decides to hire Stacy as the hospital's lawyer

episodes.theme: the cost of emotional vulnerability in professional decisions

interactions.description: both plotlines explore taking dangerous risks for someone you care about
House's feelings for Stacy directly influence his medical decision-making with her husband
Cuddy's decision to hire Stacy creates new power dynamics that will affect House's autonomy
